---
title: "29 · TD with Function Approximation: Semi-Gradient TD"
subtitle: "Derive the TD update for w via the chain rule; understand why it's semi-gradient, not full SGD."
lesson-id: "mfrl.td-with-function-approximation"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.from-table-to-function", "t6.gradient-methods"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "done"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you read on.

1. Can you state TD(0) for the *tabular* case — how each $v(s)$ is updated
   from one transition?
2. Do you know what a [gradient](/glossary.qmd#gradient) is, and how to take
   $\nabla_w$ of a scalar function of $w$?
3. Can you write a linear value approximator $\hat v(s; w) = \phi(s)^\top w$
   and say what $\phi(s)$ and $w$ are?
4. Are you comfortable with one step of gradient descent
   $w \leftarrow w - \eta \nabla_w L(w)$ on a quadratic loss?

If any answer is no, revisit the previous lesson on
[function approximation](../28-from-table-to-function/index.qmd) and the
gradient lessons before continuing.
:::

## Touch

You have a 5-state chain. The true state values $v_\pi$ are unknown to you;
you only see one transition at a time: a state $s$, a reward $r$, and the next
state $s'$.

The table approach kept one number per state. We've thrown it out. Instead we
keep just **two numbers**, $w_1$ and $w_2$, and we estimate the value of state
$s$ as

$$\hat v(s; w) = \phi_1(s)\, w_1 + \phi_2(s)\, w_2,$$

with two simple features: $\phi_1(s) = 1$ for every $s$ (a bias) and
$\phi_2(s) = s$ (the state index).

Imagine a slider that controls a learning rate $\alpha$ and a button labelled
"do one TD step". Each click reads one transition and nudges $(w_1, w_2)$ a
little. Watch the trace of $w$ in the 2-D plane.

::: {.lesson-beat}
You only see *one* transition at a time. You only have *two* knobs to turn.
But you have to make $\hat v$ correct on average across all five states.
:::

## Wonder

Sit with these. Don't write yet.

1. The TD target $r + \gamma \hat v(s'; w)$ depends on $w$. Doesn't that
   make it a *moving* target?
2. If you could see the true $v_\pi(s)$, you would know exactly what to fit.
   You can't. So what *are* you actually fitting?
3. In the tabular case, an update at state $s$ changes only the entry $v(s)$.
   With two parameters and five states, what happens to the estimates at
   *other* states when you nudge $w$?
4. Standard gradient descent minimises a fixed objective $L(w)$. Is that what
   you are doing here?

## Discover

Work through these in order. Each is small.

### Problem 1 — what we'd fit if we knew the truth

Suppose, magically, you knew $v_\pi(s)$ for every $s$. A natural thing to
optimise is the *mean square value error*:

$$J(w) \;=\; \sum_s \mu(s)\, \bigl(v_\pi(s) - \hat v(s; w)\bigr)^2,$$

where $\mu(s)$ is how often the policy visits $s$. Compute $\nabla_w J(w)$
when $\hat v(s; w) = \phi(s)^\top w$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The $v_\pi(s)$ part doesn't depend on $w$. Treat it as a constant.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Use the chain rule. The derivative of $(v_\pi(s) - \hat v(s; w))^2$ with
respect to $w$ is $2(v_\pi(s) - \hat v(s; w)) \cdot (-\nabla_w \hat v(s; w))$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$$\nabla_w J(w) \;=\; -2 \sum_s \mu(s)\,\bigl(v_\pi(s) - \hat v(s; w)\bigr)\,\nabla_w \hat v(s; w).$$

For linear FA, $\nabla_w \hat v(s; w) = \phi(s)$. So the full-gradient update
points "toward $v_\pi(s)$" weighted by the visit distribution $\mu$.
</details>

### Problem 2 — sample one transition

You don't have $v_\pi$. You have one observed transition $(s, r, s')$ drawn by
following $\pi$. Replace $v_\pi(s)$ by the TD target $r + \gamma \hat v(s'; w)$
inside the gradient from Problem 1. Drop the sum over $s$ — you only have one
sample.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
You're constructing an estimator. The visit weight $\mu(s)$ is implicit because
$s$ was sampled from $\mu$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$$w \;\leftarrow\; w + \alpha\,\bigl[r + \gamma \hat v(s'; w) - \hat v(s; w)\bigr]\,\nabla_w \hat v(s; w).$$

This is the **semi-gradient TD** update. The bracket is the
[TD error](/glossary.qmd#td-error); the trailing $\nabla_w \hat v(s; w)$ is
the only gradient we actually computed.
</details>

### Problem 3 — what was *not* differentiated

Look closely at the update from Problem 2. The TD target
$r + \gamma \hat v(s'; w)$ contains $w$. If we were doing honest gradient
descent on a fixed objective, we would also have differentiated the target
through its $w$-dependence.

What gradient term did we *drop*?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Imagine you set $L(w) = \tfrac12 (r + \gamma \hat v(s'; w) - \hat v(s; w))^2$
and computed $\nabla_w L$ by the chain rule.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The honest gradient would include $-\gamma \nabla_w \hat v(s'; w)$ inside the
bracket. Semi-gradient TD throws that term away — the target is treated as if
it did not depend on $w$. That is what the prefix *semi-* means: only half of
the gradient is taken.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
Sutton's 1988 paper introducing TD($\lambda$) noted the semi-gradient catch
but did not have a rigorous convergence proof for the function-approximation
case. The matter sat open for nearly a decade before
**Tsitsiklis and Van Roy (1997)** proved that semi-gradient TD with **linear**
function approximation, **on-policy** sampling, and a decreasing step size
converges to a unique fixed point. For non-linear FA or off-policy data, no
such guarantee exists — and divergence is real, as we'll see in
[Lesson 30](../30-q-learning-with-fa/index.qmd).
:::

### Problem 4 — when it's safe

Tsitsiklis–Van Roy gives convergence under three conditions: linear FA,
on-policy sampling, and Robbins–Monro step sizes
($\sum \alpha_t = \infty,\ \sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$). Which of these three
were already familiar from earlier lessons?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The **step-size schedule** is exactly the Robbins–Monro condition you met in
the [stochastic approximation](../20-robbins-monro/index.qmd) lesson. The
**on-policy** part says the sampling distribution $\mu$ matches the policy
whose value you're estimating — this matters because semi-gradient TD only
"chases the right fixed point" when $\mu$ is the stationary distribution of
$\pi$. **Linear FA** ensures the projection is non-expansive in the
$\mu$-weighted norm.
</details>

::: {.key-insight}
**Semi-gradient TD ignores how the target depends on $w$.** Mathematically
that means there is no fixed scalar objective $J(w)$ that this iteration
minimises by exact gradient descent. Empirically it works — and is provably
convergent for linear FA on-policy. The "semi" is the price of being able to
bootstrap: learning $\hat v(s)$ from $\hat v(s')$ before either is correct.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

For a parameterised value function $\hat v(s; w)$, the **semi-gradient TD(0)**
update on a single transition $(s, r, s')$ is

$$w \;\leftarrow\; w + \alpha\,\delta_t\,\nabla_w \hat v(s; w),
\qquad \delta_t \;=\; r + \gamma \hat v(s'; w) - \hat v(s; w).$$

It is called **semi-gradient** because it differentiates the prediction
$\hat v(s; w)$ but *not* the bootstrapped target $\hat v(s'; w)$, even though
both depend on $w$.

:::

In one line of mnemonic:

> *The error is full; the gradient is half.*

## Apply

### Worked example — 3-state chain, linear features

States $\{1, 2, 3\}$. The policy walks deterministically right; reward $r=0$
on each transition; $\gamma = 1$ for simplicity. The terminal value at state 3
is taken to be 0.

Linear FA: $\phi(s) = (1, s)^\top$, so $\hat v(s; w) = w_1 + w_2 \cdot s$.
Initial $w = (0, 0)^\top$. Step size $\alpha = 0.1$. Reward sequence: every
transition has $r = 0$ in this episode-free toy.

Suppose we observe the transition $s=1,\ r=0,\ s'=2$.

1. $\hat v(1; w) = 0$, $\hat v(2; w) = 0$.
2. $\delta = 0 + 1 \cdot 0 - 0 = 0$.

So after one zero-reward transition, $w$ doesn't move. Now consider a chain
where the terminal transition $s=2,\ r=1,\ s'=3$ pays out, and $\hat v(3; w)$
is forced to 0 (terminal).

1. $\hat v(2; w) = 0$, $\hat v(3; w) = 0$.
2. $\delta = 1 + 0 - 0 = 1$.
3. $\nabla_w \hat v(2; w) = \phi(2) = (1, 2)^\top$.
4. $w \leftarrow (0, 0) + 0.1 \cdot 1 \cdot (1, 2)$ $= (0.1,\ 0.2)$.

Now $\hat v(1; w) = 0.1 + 0.2 \cdot 1 = 0.3$, $\hat v(2; w) = 0.5$. **One
update at state 2 has changed the estimate at state 1 too** — that's
generalisation by feature sharing, the whole point of FA.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** With $\hat v(s; w) = w_1 + w_2 s$, write down $\nabla_w \hat v(s; w)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Differentiate with respect to $w_1$ and $w_2$ separately.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\partial \hat v / \partial w_1 = 1,\ \partial \hat v / \partial w_2 = s$, so
$\nabla_w \hat v(s; w) = (1, s)^\top = \phi(s)$.
</details>

**W2.** State the TD error $\delta_t$ on a transition $(s, r, s')$ for the
linear FA in W1, with $\gamma = 0.9$ and current $w = (1, 0.5)^\top$, given
$s=2$, $r=3$, $s'=4$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Plug into $\delta = r + \gamma \hat v(s'; w) - \hat v(s; w)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\hat v(2; w) = 1 + 0.5 \cdot 2 = 2$. $\hat v(4; w) = 1 + 0.5 \cdot 4 = 3$.
$\delta = 3 + 0.9 \cdot 3 - 2 = 3.7$.
</details>

**W3.** Using W2, compute the next $w$ with $\alpha = 0.1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Apply $w \leftarrow w + \alpha \delta \nabla_w \hat v(s; w)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Update direction: $\delta \cdot \phi(s) = 3.7 \cdot (1, 2) = (3.7,\ 7.4)$.
New $w = (1, 0.5) + 0.1 \cdot (3.7, 7.4) = (1.37,\ 1.24)$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Repeat the chain example from "Worked example" but starting from
$w = (1, 0)^\top$, observing the same single transition $(s=2, r=1, s'=3,
\gamma=1, \alpha=0.1)$. Show the new $w$ and compare $\hat v(1)$ before and
after.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Compute $\hat v(2; w)$ first; then $\delta$, then the update.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Don't forget the bias feature $\phi_1(s) = 1$ in $\phi(2) = (1, 2)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\hat v(2; w) = 1$, $\hat v(3; w) = 1$ (terminal value forced to 0 in the
worked example, but here we treat $s=3$ as a normal state for illustration —
adjust as needed). With $\hat v(3) = 0$ for a true terminal:
$\delta = 1 + 0 - 1 = 0$, no update. With $\hat v(3) = 1$ (no terminal
forcing): $\delta = 1 + 1 - 1 = 1$; new $w = (1, 0) + 0.1 \cdot 1 \cdot (1, 2)
= (1.1, 0.2)$. Before: $\hat v(1) = 1$. After: $\hat v(1) = 1.1 + 0.2 = 1.3$.
The estimate at state 1 moved even though we didn't visit state 1.
</details>

**P2.** A student claims: "Semi-gradient TD is just SGD on the loss
$L(w) = \tfrac12 \delta^2$." Refute this claim in one short paragraph.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
What does honest SGD on $L(w) = \tfrac12 (r + \gamma \hat v(s'; w) -
\hat v(s; w))^2$ produce when you do the chain rule?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Honest SGD on that $L$ would yield
$\nabla_w L = \delta \cdot (\gamma \nabla_w \hat v(s'; w) - \nabla_w \hat v(s; w))$,
which contains an extra $\gamma \nabla_w \hat v(s'; w)$ term that semi-gradient
TD discards. So semi-gradient TD is not SGD on this loss. (The variant that
*does* keep the target's gradient is called the **residual gradient** method;
it has its own pathologies — slower learning and a tendency to find the wrong
fixed point.)
</details>

**P3.** Suppose you have $|S| = 5$ states and use a **one-hot** feature vector
$\phi(s) = e_s$ — the $s$-th standard basis vector. Show that the
semi-gradient TD update reduces to the *tabular* TD(0) update.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
For one-hot features, $w$ has 5 entries and $\hat v(s; w) = w_s$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Compute $\nabla_w \hat v(s; w)$ when $\phi(s) = e_s$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\nabla_w \hat v(s; w) = e_s$, so the update only changes the $s$-th entry of
$w$: $w_s \leftarrow w_s + \alpha \delta$. Since $\hat v(s; w) = w_s$ this
*is* the tabular TD(0) update. One-hot FA is the special case where
"function approximation" approximates nothing — every state has its own
parameter and there is no generalisation. So tabular TD is a special case of
semi-gradient TD.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** State (without proof) why semi-gradient TD with **non-linear** FA
loses the convergence guarantee. Give one intuitive reason.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The Tsitsiklis–Van Roy proof relies on the fact that linear FA defines a
non-expansive projection in a particular norm.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The proof rests on showing that the composition of the Bellman operator with
the *projection onto the linear subspace* is a contraction in the
$\mu$-weighted norm. For non-linear FA the projection isn't a true projection,
isn't necessarily a contraction, and the composed operator can have multiple
fixed points or none. Intuitively: a neural network's representation can
*move* under updates, so chasing $\hat v(s'; w)$ can drag the function in a
direction that doesn't decrease error elsewhere. We see this fail in
[Lesson 30](../30-q-learning-with-fa/index.qmd).
</details>

**S2.** Re-derive the semi-gradient TD update for a **non-linear** FA
$\hat v(s; w) = \tanh(w^\top \phi(s))$. What changes? What stays the same?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Only the gradient changes; the structure of the update does not.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The update is
$w \leftarrow w + \alpha \delta \nabla_w \hat v(s; w)$
with $\nabla_w \hat v(s; w) = (1 - \tanh^2(w^\top \phi(s)))\,\phi(s)$ by the
[chain rule](/glossary.qmd#chain-rule). The shape — TD error times the gradient
of the prediction — is unchanged. What changes is that the convergence theorem
no longer applies.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Semi-gradient TD is SGD on the squared TD error."** No — see P2. The
   target depends on $w$ but is treated as if it doesn't. Calling this SGD is
   a category error.
2. **"Semi-gradient is just sloppy."** Also no. Including the missing target
   gradient (the *residual gradient* method) gives valid SGD, but it tends to
   converge to the wrong place when the model is mis-specified, and learns
   much more slowly. Semi-gradient is the empirically better choice; it just
   has weaker theory.
3. **"Linear FA is a toy."** It's not — linear FA over hand-crafted or learned
   features (tile-coding, RBFs, the penultimate layer of a frozen network) is
   the regime in which semi-gradient TD is *provably* well-behaved. Modern
   deep RL chooses non-linear FA and accepts the theoretical loss in exchange
   for representational power.
:::

### Takeaways

1. **Linear FA + on-policy ⇒ semi-gradient TD converges** to a unique fixed
   point (Tsitsiklis–Van Roy 1997). Outside that regime, all bets are off.
2. The update is **TD error × prediction gradient**:
   $w \leftarrow w + \alpha \delta \nabla_w \hat v(s; w)$. Memorise this
   shape — every variant in this track plugs different pieces in.
3. The "semi" matters: **the bootstrapped target's $w$-dependence is ignored.**
   Honest SGD on the squared TD error gives a different (and worse-performing)
   algorithm.

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you explain to a friend why semi-gradient TD is not exact SGD on a
   fixed objective?
2. Did you derive the semi-gradient TD update yourself in *Discover* before
   reading the *Name* beat?
3. Could you implement one step of semi-gradient TD on a 5-state chain with
   a 2-feature linear approximator from scratch?

- **3 yes:** continue to [Lesson 30 — Q-Learning with FA and the Deadly
  Triad](../30-q-learning-with-fa/index.qmd).
- **2 yes:** redo S2, then move on.
- **0–1 yes:** re-derive the update in *Discover* by hand.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[mfrl.from-table-to-function](../28-from-table-to-function/index.qmd)** —
  parameterised value functions $\hat v(s; w)$.
- **t6.gradient-methods** — basic SGD and the Robbins–Monro step-size
  schedule.

### What this unlocks

- **[Q-Learning with Function Approximation](../30-q-learning-with-fa/index.qmd)** —
  the off-policy variant where things can go wrong.
- **[DQN](../31-deep-q-network/index.qmd)** — the engineering fixes that
  defeat the deadly triad.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Almost every modern RL agent that estimates a value function
runs some form of semi-gradient update — DQN, A2C, PPO, SAC. The "semi"
catch reappears every time someone proposes a new value-based algorithm and
has to argue convergence informally.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** The 1997 Tsitsiklis–Van Roy paper is one of the most-cited
results in the theory of approximate dynamic programming. Its conditions
delineate the safe regime, and every subsequent theoretical paper on RL with
function approximation cites it.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view of reinforcement learning that this
track expands into 38 lessons.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- Source: Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement Learning*
  (Springer 2025), §8 (function approximation, semi-gradient TD). Cite
  freely; never transcribe Zhao's prose.
- Touchstones honoured: Russian school problem-first sequencing (Discover
  derives the update before *Name* states it), Better Explained "Aha!"
  framing (the key-insight callout: the error is full, the gradient is half).
:::
